content:
The simple explanation of the C10K problem, event loops, epoll, and why Nginx can handle massive traffic spikes while traditional servers struggle.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Imagine building a web application that normally serves 20 users a day.
Everything works perfectly.
Then one day, your product gets mentioned on social media, goes viral, and suddenly 10,000 people try to use it at the same time.
Within minutes, users start seeing errors. Pages load slowly. Some requests never complete.
Most people immediately blame the server hardware.
“We need more RAM.”
“We need a bigger CPU.”
“We need a faster network.”
But in many cases, the real problem isn’t the hardware at all.
The problem is how the software handles connections.
Let’s explore why traditional web servers struggled with large traffic spikes and how Nginx changed everything.

What Is a Connection?
Before understanding scalability, we need to understand what a connection actually is.
When your browser visits a website, it first creates a communication channel with the server.
This communication channel is called a connection.
Through that connection:

• The browser sends requests

• The server sends responses

• Multiple requests can travel over the same connection

• The connection may remain open even when no data is being transferred
A common misconception is that one request equals one connection.
Not necessarily.
A single connection can handle multiple requests over time, and it often stays open while waiting for future communication.
This detail becomes extremely important when traffic grows.

The Old Way: One Thread Per Connection
In the late 1990s and early 2000s, many web servers followed a simple model:
One connection = One thread
Every time a user connected:

• The server created a new thread (or process)

• That thread was assigned to the user

• The thread remained alive for the entire lifetime of the connection
At first, this sounds reasonable.
If 100 users connect, the server creates 100 threads.
No problem.
But what happens when 10,000 users connect?
Now the server needs:

• 10,000 threads

• 10,000 memory stacks

• 10,000 scheduling operations
Things quickly become expensive.

Why 10,000 Threads Become a Disaster
Every thread consumes memory.
Even with aggressive optimization, a thread may require around 1 MB of memory.
That means:

• 1,000 threads ≈ 1 GB RAM

• 10,000 threads ≈ 10 GB RAM
And that’s before your application performs any actual work.
The server is spending enormous amounts of memory simply keeping connections alive.
Memory isn’t the only problem.
The CPU also suffers.

The Hidden Cost: Context Switching
A CPU core can execute only one task at a time.
To create the illusion of parallel execution, the operating system rapidly switches between threads.
This process is called context switching.
Every switch requires the operating system to:

• Save the state of the current thread

• Load the state of another thread

• Resume execution
When a server has thousands of active threads, the CPU spends a huge portion of its time managing thread switches rather than serving users.
Eventually, performance collapses.
This challenge became famous as the:
The C10K Problem
“C” stands for concurrent.
“10K” stands for 10,000 connections.
The challenge was simple:
How can a server efficiently handle 10,000 simultaneous connections?
For years, this was one of the biggest scalability problems in web infrastructure.

Why Traditional Threads Waste Resources
Here’s the surprising part.
Most connections aren’t doing anything.
A connection spends most of its life waiting.
Waiting for:

• A user action

• A database response

• A file read

• Network transmission
While waiting, the dedicated thread remains occupied.
It cannot help another user.
It simply sits there consuming memory and system resources.
This inefficiency is exactly what Nginx was designed to solve.

How Nginx Thinks Differently
Instead of assigning one thread to every connection, Nginx uses an event-driven architecture.
Rather than creating thousands of threads, a single worker process can manage thousands of connections.
The key idea is simple:
Only spend resources on connections that are actively doing something.
If a connection is idle, ignore it until activity occurs.
This dramatically reduces memory usage and CPU overhead.

Nginx Architecture: Master and Worker Processes
When Nginx starts, it creates:
Master Process
The master process is responsible for:

• Reading configuration files

• Opening network ports

• Managing worker processes

• Handling reloads and upgrades
The master process does very little traffic handling itself.
Worker Processes
Workers handle actual network traffic.
A common recommendation is:
One worker per CPU core
For example:

• 4 CPU cores → 4 workers

• 8 CPU cores → 8 workers
This reduces unnecessary context switching and allows workers to run efficiently.

How Does Nginx Monitor Thousands of Connections?
Imagine trying to manually check 10,000 connections every second.
That would be incredibly inefficient.
Instead, Nginx delegates this responsibility to the Linux kernel.
The kernel already sees all incoming network traffic.
So it knows exactly which connections are active and which are idle.
Nginx simply asks:
“Tell me when something important happens.”
This is where epoll comes in.

What Is Epoll?
Epoll is a Linux mechanism designed for high-performance network applications.
It allows Nginx to efficiently monitor massive numbers of connections.
Conceptually, epoll maintains two groups:
Interest List
Connections Nginx wants the kernel to watch.
Ready List
Connections that currently have activity and need attention.
When a new connection arrives:

• Nginx registers it with epoll

• The connection enters the interest list

• The kernel monitors it
Nothing else happens until activity occurs.

A Real Example
Let’s say a user opens your web application.
The connection gets registered with epoll.
At this moment:

• The connection exists

• Nothing is happening

• No resources are wasted
Now the user clicks the “Orders” page.
A request is sent.
The kernel immediately notices incoming data.
The connection is placed into the ready list.
Now Nginx knows this connection requires attention.
The worker processes the request and sends back the response.
Afterward, the connection returns to its idle state.
The cycle repeats only when new activity occurs.

The Event Loop Explained
At the heart of Nginx lies something called the event loop.
The worker process continuously performs the same steps:

• Ask epoll for active connections

• Process those connections

• Return to waiting
If no connections need attention, the worker simply sleeps.
It consumes almost no CPU resources.
Unlike traditional servers, Nginx does not constantly poll every connection.
Instead, it waits until the operating system wakes it up.
This design is one of the biggest reasons Nginx scales so efficiently.

The One Weakness of Event Loops
Event loops are powerful, but they have a weakness.
A worker should never become stuck on a slow operation.
Imagine a user requests a large video stored on an old mechanical hard drive.
The disk takes several seconds to retrieve the file.
If the worker waits for the disk operation to complete:

• Thousands of other connections must wait

• Response times increase

• Performance drops
The entire worker becomes blocked.
This creates a bottleneck.

How Thread Pools Solve the Problem
Modern systems avoid this issue through thread pools.
When Nginx encounters a slow task:

• It does not perform the operation directly

• The task is sent to a background worker thread

• The main event loop remains free
While the background thread reads the file:

• The Nginx worker continues serving other users

• New requests are processed normally

• Performance remains responsive
Once the slow operation finishes, the result is returned to the event loop.
The response is then delivered to the user.
This approach combines the efficiency of event-driven architecture with the practicality of background processing.

Nginx Is Not Your Application
One important detail is often misunderstood.
Nginx does not typically execute your application code.
Instead, it acts as a reverse proxy.
For example:

• User sends a request

• Nginx receives it

• Nginx forwards it to a backend application

• Backend generates a response

• Nginx sends the response back to the user
The backend could be:

• Node.js

• Python

• Java

• Go

• PHP

• .NET
Nginx sits between users and your application.
This makes your infrastructure faster, safer, and easier to scale.

Nginx as a Load Balancer
Because Nginx already sits in front of your application, it can also distribute traffic.
Instead of running one backend server, you can run many.
Nginx automatically spreads requests across them.
For example:

• Server A

• Server B

• Server C

• Server D
Rather than overwhelming a single machine, traffic gets distributed across the entire cluster.
This is known as load balancing.
It is one of the core techniques behind modern scalable web applications.

Why Nginx Became the Industry Standard
The traditional “one thread per connection” model worked when websites had small amounts of traffic.
But it breaks down under large-scale workloads.
Nginx introduced a fundamentally different approach.
Instead of dedicating resources to idle connections, it only acts when work actually needs to be done.
The result is:

• Lower memory consumption

• Fewer context switches

• Better CPU utilization

• Higher scalability

• Faster response times
This event-driven architecture is why Nginx can efficiently manage tens of thousands of concurrent connections while consuming a fraction of the resources required by older server designs.
If you’ve ever wondered how modern websites survive sudden traffic spikes, the answer often starts with one simple idea:
Don’t waste resources on connections that are doing nothing.
